Title: Control of carotenoid biosynthesis through a heme-based cis-trans isomerase

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential for plant development and survival; essential nutrients for human health
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: central desaturation/isomerization module of carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: midstream; required step between ζ-carotene desaturation intermediates and downstream carotenoid formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms the substrate required by the following biosynthetic pathway
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: integral membrane heme b-containing isomerase; predicted seven transmembrane domains; chloroplast transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes cis-to-trans isomerization of the 15-15′ C=C bond in 9,15,9′-cis-ζ-carotene to produce 9,9′-cis-ζ-carotene, the substrate for downstream carotenoid biosynthetic enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for carotenoid pathway flux; promotes carotenoid biosynthesis by converting the ζ-carotene intermediate into the downstream-utilizable isomer; mutations or loss of activity block carotenoid end-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purified in vitro enzyme assay with substrate-containing liposomes; E. coli complementation with carotenoid profiling by HPLC; site-directed mutagenesis; chloroplast GFP localization and in vitro import; metal/heme analyses by ICP-OES, heme stain, pyridine hemochrome, UV-Vis, EPR, and MCD
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control/gatekeeper step under redox control, but not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Z-ISO is a bona fide enzyme that independently catalyzes cis-to-trans isomerization of the 15-15′ double bond of 9,15,9′-cis-ζ-carotene. 2. Enzymatic activity requires reducing conditions, demonstrating redox control of this carotenoid biosynthetic step. 3. Z-ISO is a chloroplast integral membrane protein containing heme b and undergoing redox-dependent ligand switching. 4. Conserved residues H150 and H266 are required for heme binding and isomerase activity, whereas C263 is not catalytic but contributes to heme ligation/binding. 5. The study proposes Z-ISO functions as a gatekeeper controlling carotenoid pathway flux in response to plastid redox state.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Reducing conditions generated by sodium dithionite are required for in vitro activity; dark conditions used to prevent photoisomerization; plastid redox state and potentially nitric oxide are discussed as physiological regulators
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S promoter for transient Z-ISO-GFP expression in maize protoplasts (constitutive)
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strategic target for metabolic engineering or breeding to improve carotenoid accumulation and nutritional quality; manipulation of Z-ISO activity or its redox regulation may help optimize carotenoid flux and resilience under changing environmental conditions; should consider redox dependence and plastid context
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because Z-ISO controls pathway flux in plastids, altering its activity may affect photosynthesis-related physiology, stress signaling, and broader plastid redox homeostasis; specific yield penalties not reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Z-ISO exists in a ~480 kDa protein complex in chloroplasts. The enzyme contains one heme b cofactor and displays reversible His/His versus His/Cys axial ligation depending on redox state. The reduced ferrous state is active and can bind exogenous ligands such as CO and NO, supporting a catalytic heme role rather than simple electron transfer.
